symbols: three angels — heavenly messengers and ministers of God's will
flowing garments — celestial grace and spiritual motion
lunette format — the arched space above an altar or doorway, connecting earth and heaven
intimate grouping — the communion and harmony of heavenly beings
description: A sepia-toned preparatory drawing in a lunette (half-moon) format showing three winged angelic figures seated together in intimate conversation. The figures have flowing robes and graceful poses, leaning toward one another. Their wings are partially visible. The drawing is rendered in loose, confident ink or wash strokes on aged paper. A small circular stamp or seal is visible at the bottom center. The overall composition is fluid and dynamic, capturing a moment of heavenly communion.
theological_description: Three angels gathered in intimate communion evoke the rich biblical theology of angelic ministry. Angels serve as God's messengers and ministers — 'Are not all angels ministering spirits sent to serve those who will inherit salvation?' (Hebrews 1:14). The grouping of three angels specifically recalls the visit of three heavenly visitors to Abraham at the oaks of Mamre (Genesis 18:1-2), an event which early Christian theology interpreted as a theophany prefiguring the Trinity. The intimate, conversational posture of these figures suggests the harmony of heaven — the concord of beings perfectly aligned with God's will. Their presence in a lunette format, typically placed above an altar, positions them as intermediaries between the earthly worship below and the heavenly reality above, fulfilling the vision of Jacob's ladder where 'the angels of God were ascending and descending' (Genesis 28:12).

scripture_refs.reason: Three visitors appearing to Abraham at Mamre — the classic Old Testament grouping of three heavenly beings
Angels as ministering spirits — these celestial beings in service of God's purposes
Angels ascending and descending — the connection between heaven and earth these figures embody
